name: proxypypi
vendor: Richard Jones (individual maintainer; original author of the legacy PyPI/cheeseshop codebase)
perf_notes: No published benchmarks (throughput/latency/memory). Design goal is faster repeat installs via local caching after the first fetch. To survive pip's short connection timeout, it streams a background fetch of the upstream file to the client rather than downloading-then-serving; v1.2.2 removed the partial-download path for missing external files. Single synchronous process; not optimized for high concurrency.
scale_notes: Single-node, single-process tool backed by one local filesystem cache directory. No HA, clustering, multi-node, or stateless horizontal-scaling story. Optionally runs as a background daemon (PID/log/console-output files + start/stop control commands). Explicitly positioned as a simpler alternative to devpi-server, and the README itself recommends using devpi instead. Effectively abandoned since 2013; its Bitbucket Mercurial repo is gone (404 after Bitbucket's 2020 Mercurial sunset).
summary: A tiny, database-free, on-demand caching/pull-through PyPI proxy from 2013 that lazily fetches and rewrites package files to local references and can also serve private packages dropped into its cache directory.
extras: Streaming background fetch keeps pip's short-timeout connection alive while the upstream file is retrieved (its defining trick)
Rewrites BOTH PyPI-hosted and externally-hosted download URLs to local references, handling the externally-hosted-file era of pre-2014 PyPI
Drop-in private package hosting: just place distribution files in the cache root (package-named subdirs optional, not required)
Database-free by design (filesystem only) explicitly to be simpler to deploy than devpi-server
Background daemon mode with PID file (-P), log file (-l), console-output file (-o) and daemon start/stop control commands; cache dir set via -d
Self-deprecated: README warns 'Don't use this software. Use devpi instead!'
No cache upgrade/refresh of already-cached packages ('This isn't done yet') so cached versions are effectively pinned forever
